---
title: "Dates and Times I"
subtitle: "Storing, creating, displaying, and converting time."
format: stat133slides-revealjs
revealjs-plugins:
  - drop
chalkboard: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, error = TRUE)
library(tidyverse)
```


## Agenda

1) How does a computer __Store__ dates and times?

2) How do we __Create__ dates and times?

3) How do we __Get and Set__ them?


# Store

## Unix Time

. . .

A method to measure time as the number of seconds[^1] that have elapsed since midnight UTC[^2] on 1 January 1970, the [Unix epoch]{.mdlit}.

. . .

Examples:

| Date Time (UTC)      | Unix Time |
|----------------------|-----------|
| 12:00 am Jan 1, 1970 | 0         |
| 12:01 am Jan 1, 1970 | 60        |

[^1]: excluding leap-seconds
[^2]: Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)


## Unix Time {auto-animate="true"}

A method to measure time as the number of seconds[^1] that have elapsed since midnight UTC[^2] on 1 January 1970, the [Unix epoch]{.mdlit}.

Examples:

| Date Time (UTC)      | Unix Time |
|----------------------|-----------|
| 12:00 am Jan 1, 1970 | 0         |
| 12:01 am Jan 1, 1970 | 60        |
| 12:01 am Jan 2, 1970 | 86460     |

[^1]: excluding leap-seconds
[^2]: Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)


## Unix Time {auto-animate="true"}

A method to measure time as the number of seconds[^1] that have elapsed since midnight UTC[^2] on 1 January 1970, the [Unix epoch]{.mdlit}.

Examples:

| Date Time (UTC)      | Unix Time |
|----------------------|-----------|
| 12:00 am Jan 1, 1970 | 0         |
| 12:01 am Jan 1, 1970 | 60        |
| 12:01 am Jan 2, 1970 | 86460     |
| 7 pm Mar 16, 2026 (today at noon) | 1773687600     |

[^1]: excluding leap-seconds
[^2]: Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)


## Unix Time {auto-animate="true"}

A method to measure time as the number of seconds[^1] that have elapsed since midnight UTC[^2] on 1 January 1970, the [Unix epoch]{.mdlit}.

Examples:

| Date Time (UTC)      | Unix Time |
|----------------------|-----------|
| 12:00 am Jan 1, 1970 | 0         |
| 12:01 am Jan 1, 1970 | 60        |
| 12:01 am Jan 2, 1970 | 86460     |
| 7 pm Mar 16, 2026 (today at noon) | 1773687600     |
| 1:33 pm Dec 31, 1933 | -1136131200 |

[^1]: excluding leap-seconds
[^2]: Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)


##

<iframe width="1000" height="500" src="https://www.unixtimestamp.com/" title="Unix Timestamp"></iframe>

\

<https://www.unixtimestamp.com/>


## An R vector for date-times

[POSIXct]{.bold-hilit} (Portable Operating System Interface Calendar Time) is an atomic vector to store date-times that is built on top of a double vector.

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
now <- Sys.time()
now
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
class(now)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
typeof(now)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
# unix time (when rendering slides)
unclass(now)
```


## Why not just use a double vector?

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
# it prints as a legible string
now
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
# while respecting arithmetic
now + 1
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
# and other sensible operations
round(now)
```

. . .


```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
# and makes it easy to use other formats
format(now, "%m/%d/%y")
```


# Creating

## Creating date-time vectors

Use `as.POSIXct()` to create a date-time vector from a numeric (in Unix Time) or a string in a particular format.

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
as.POSIXct(x = 60, tz = "UTC")
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
as.POSIXct(x = "1970-01-01 00:01:00", tz = "UTC")
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
as.POSIXct(x = "Jan 1, 1970 12:01 am", 
           format = "%b %d, %Y %I:%M %p",  
           tz = "UTC")
```

. . .

> Formats: <https://r4ds.hadley.nz/datetimes.html#tbl-date-formats>


## Quick aside

In addition to a `POSIXct` vector, you can store simpler date data in a `Date` vector, created with `as.Date()`.

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
halloween <- as.Date("2000-10-31")
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
class(halloween)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
typeof(halloween)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
halloween
```


## Quick aside (cont'd)

In addition to a `POSIXct` vector, you can store simpler date data in a `Date` vector, created with `as.Date()`.

```{r}
as.Date("03-16-2026", format = "%m-%d-%Y")
```

. . .

\

```{r}
as.Date("2026-March-16", format = "%Y-%B-%d")
```

. . .

\

```{r}
as.Date("2026-Mar-16", format = "%Y-%b-%d")
```


## {auto-animate=true}

![](images/tidyverse-hex-logo.png){fig-align="center"}


## {auto-animate=true}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](images/tidyverse-hex-logo.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](images/lubridate-hex.svg)
:::
:::


## Creating with `"lubridate"`

. . .

```{r}
#| eval: false
library(tidyverse)  # contains "lubridate"
```

. . .

`"lubridate"` has many functions that can parse the year (y), month (m), day (d), hour (h), minute (m), and second (s) components of a date-time string.

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
mdy_hm("Jan 1, 1970 12:01 am")
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
dmy_hm("1-1-1970 12:01")
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
x <- dmy_hm("1-1-1970 12:01")
class(x)
```


# Getting and Setting

## Getting

You can extract components of the date-time object using `year()`, `month()`, `week()`, `day()`, `wday()`, `hour()`, etc.

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
year(now)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
day(now)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
wday(now)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
wday(now, label = TRUE)
```


## Getting

You can extract components of the date-time object using `year()`, `month()`, `week()`, `day()`, `wday()`, `hour()`, etc.

```{r}
year(now)
```


```{r}
day(now)
```


```{r}
wday(now)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
wday(now, label = TRUE, abbr = FALSE)
```


## Setting

You can set those same components in an existing date-time object using change-in-place notation.

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
year(now) <- 2025
now
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
wday(now, label = TRUE, abbr = FALSE)
```


## Your Turn

Question: Write the (lines) of code necessary to determine:

1) On which day of the week will New Years Eve fall this year?

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: false
wday("2026-12-31")
```

2) What day of the week was last year's US Independence Day?

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: false
wday("2025-07-04")
```
